coredata.dc:title: YAP/TFRC/ALOXE3 signaling is involved in ferroptosis and neuroinflammation triggered by amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Tianshu
Wang, Xinyu
Chen, Min
Zhang, Xiaomeng
Zhang, Jixiang
Fan, Xuqing
Yu, Yunbo
Zheng, Wenxin
Maitiyv, Supu
Wāng, Yán
Kong, Lu
Tang, Meng
coredata.dc:description: Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been attracting extensive interests in a widespread application, but the little information on the risk assessments of GQDs could impose a restriction on their contributions in the neuroscience. In this study, the intranasal administration of amino group functionalized GQDs (A-GQDs) was observed to enhance the number of neuronal cell death, trigger ferroptosis activities, i.e. ferrous iron overload and lipid peroxidation as well as cause inflammatory reactions in hippocampus. Meanwhile, these adverse effects were found in BV2 microglial cells treated with A-GQDs, which were all attenuated by a ferroptosis-specific inhibitor ferrostain-1 (Fer-1). The inflammatory responses to A-GQDs in BV2 cells were evidenced as the increased secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokine TNF-α that was attributed to the high mobility group box 1 (HMGB1) released from ferroptosis-occurred cells. The nuclear transference of yes-associated protein (YAP), an effector in Hippo signaling pathway, regulated by the phosphorylation of p38 mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) was then found to play a critical role in A-GQDs triggering ferroptosis through transcriptionally mediating transferrin receptor protein (TFRC) and arachidonate lipoxygenase 3 (ALOXE3) to increase levels of ferrous iron and lipid peroxides, respectively. The findings not only highlight the importance of risk assessments on QDs containing low toxic component, but also provide toxic biomarkers for the interventions of GQDs in the brain.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hippocampus
Microglia
Iron overload
Lipid peroxidation
Hippo signaling pathway
HMGB1
p38 MAPK

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: A-GQDs triggered ferroptosis and caused neuroinflammation in the hippocampus of mice
A-GQDs triggered ferroptosis to increase inflammatory cytokines secretion in the cultured BV2 microglial cells
The YAP1 nuclear translocation mediated by p38 MAPK phosphorylation took part in the regulation of A-GQDs inducing ferroptosis and inflammation both in vitro and in vivo
The nuclear transference of YAP1 caused by A-GQDs activated transferrin receptor protein (TFRC) to increase ferrous iron level in BV2 cells
The nuclear transference of YAP1 caused by A-GQDs activated ALOXE3 to elicit lipid peroxidation in BV2 cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The physicochemical characterization of A-GQDs. (a) The TEM images with HRTEM images inserted; (b) The size distribution; (c) The AFM topography; (d) The height distribution; (e) The FT-IR spectrum; (f, g) The hydrous particle diameters; (h) The UV–vis absorption spectra; (i, j) The excitation and emission spectra at λem = 460 nm and λex = 380 nm.

Ferroptosis changes and inflammatory reactions responded to A-GQDs were alleviated by Fer-1 in the hippocampus. (a, b) Representative images and the quantitative results of TUNEL positive cells indicating dead cells in hippocampus (scale bar: 100 µm for Hippo and 20 µm for the rest). The TUNEL positive cells indicated by arrows showed tan color; (c) Levels of ferrous iron in hippocampus; (d, e) Representative IHC images and IHC scores of 4-HNE expressions in hippocampus (scale bar: 100 µm for Hippo and 20 µm for the rest). Arrows indicated increased 4-HNE expressions showed tan color; (f) Representative histological images of hippocampus stained with H&E (scale bar: 100 µm for Hippo and 20 µm for the rest). Arrows indicated infiltration of inflammatory cells. (g) The inflammatory infiltration score in hippocampus; (h, i) Levels of HBMG1 and TNF-α in hippocampus. Data are showed as mean+SD of three independent experiments. (* P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001).

A-GQDs triggered ferroptosis in BV2 cells. (a) Representative TEM images showing the ultrastructure of cells. Red arrows indicate dilated endoplasmic reticulum, yellow arrows indicate broken mitochondrial ridge, blue arrows indicate shrinking mitochondria. N (nucleus), M (mitochondria), ER (Endoplasmic reticulum); (b, c) Representative flow cytometer dot plots and quantitative results of necrotic and apoptotic cells using PI/Annexin V dyes in cells; (d, e) Representative fluorescent images of intracellular ferrous iron levels in cells (scale bar: 20 µm) and quantitative results of mean fluorescence intensity. The nucleus showed blue using DAPI, and ferrous iron accumulation showed orange using FerroOrange dye; (f, g, h, i) Representative FITC fluorescence histogram plots and quantitative results of mean fluorescence intensity of cytosolic ROS and lipid ROS in cells using DCFH-DA dye and C11-BODIPY581/591, respectively; (j, k) The MDA content and GSH/GSSG ratio in cells; (l, m, n, o) Protein expressions of GPx4 and SCL7A11 in cells with/without pre-treated with Fer-1 and quantitative results of ratio of gray value. Data are showed as mean+SD of three independent experiments. (* P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001).

A-GQDs induced ferroptosis relevant inflammatory reactions in BV2 cells. (a) Heatmap of differentially expressed genes associated with inflammation caused by 100 µg/mL A-GQDs in cells using microarray analysis; (b, c) Levels of HBMG1 and TNF-α in cells; (d, e) Levels of HMGB1 and TNF-α in cells pre-treated with Fer-1; (f) Levels of TNF-α in cells pre-treated with glycyrrhizin. Data are showed as mean+SD of three independent experiments. (* P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001).

The Hippo/YAP signaling was involved in ferroptosis caused by A-GQDs in BV2 cells. (a) KEGG signaling pathways enriched by differentially expressed genes caused by 100 µg/mL A-GQDs in cells using microarray analysis; (b) Heatmap of critical genes in the hippo signaling pathway; (c, d) Protein expressions of p-YAP1 and YAP1 in cytoplasm and nucleus of cells and quantitative results of ratio of gray value; (e, f) Representative IF images of YAP1 expressions in cells (scale bar: 20 µm) and colocalized pixels map images with scatter plots inserted, and quantitative results of Rcoloc indicating Pearson’s for image above thresholds; (g, h) Representative PE fluorescence histogram plots and percentages of PI-positive cells (necrosis cells) in cells transfected with LV-YAP1-siRNA-GFP using PI dye; (i, j) Representative PE fluorescence histogram plots and quantitative results of mean fluorescence intensity of cytosolic oxidative stress in cells transfected with LV-YAP1-siRNA-GFP using CellROX Orange dye; (k, l) Representative fluorescent images of intracellular ferrous iron levels in cells transfected with LV- YAP1-siRNA-GFP (scale bar: 20 µm) and quantitative results of mean fluorescence intensity. The nucleus showed blue using DAPI, and ferrous iron accumulation showed orange using FerroOrange dye; (m) Levels of TNF-α in cells transfected with LV-YAP1-siRNA-GFP; (n, o) Necrosis cells (p, q) Intracellular ferrous iron levels (r, s) Cytosolic oxidative stress, (t) Levels of TNF-α in cells transfected with LV-YAP1-cDNA-GFP and pre-treated with Fer-1. Data are showed as mean+SD of three independent experiments. (* P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001).

YAP1 played a critical role in A-GQDs triggering ferroptosis and inflammatory reactions in the hippocampus. (a, b) Representative images and the quantitative results of TUNEL positive cells indicating dead cells in hippocampus microinjected with LV-YAP1-siRNA-GFP (scale bar: 100 µm for Hippo and 20 µm for the rest). The TUNEL positive cells indicated by arrows showed tan color; (c) Levels of ferrous iron in hippocampus microinjected with LV-YAP1-siRNA-GFP; (d, e) Representative IHC images and IHC scores of 4-HNE expressions in hippocampus microinjected with LV-YAP1-siRNA-GFP (scale bar: 100 µm for Hippo and 20 µm for the rest). Arrows indicated increased 4-HNE expressions displayed tan color; (f) Representative histological images of hippocampus stained with H&E (scale bar: 100 µm for Hippo and 20 µm for the rest). Arrows indicated infiltration of inflammatory cells. (g) The inflammatory infiltration score in hippocampus microinjected with LV-YAP1-siRNA-GFP; (h, i) Levels of HBMG1 and TNF-α in hippocampus microinjected with LV-YAP1-siRNA-GFP. Data are showed as mean+SD of three independent experiments. (* P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001).

The phosphorylation p38 MAPK participated in YAP1 nuclear transference to trigger ferroptosis in BV2 cells exposed to A-GQDs. (a) Heatmap of critical genes in the MAPK signaling pathway; (b, c) Protein expressions of p-p38 and p38 in cells and quantitative results of ratio of gray value; (d, e) Protein expressions of p-YAP1 and YAP1 in cytoplasm and nucleus of cells pre-treated with SB203580 and quantitative results of ratio of gray value; (f, g) Representative flow cytometer dot plots and quantitative results of necrotic and apoptotic cells using PI/Annexin V dyes in cells pre-treated with SB203580; (h, i) Representative fluorescent images of intracellular ferrous iron levels in cells pre-treated with SB203580 (scale bar: 20 µm) and quantitative results of mean fluorescence intensity. The nucleus showed blue using DAPI, and ferrous iron accumulation showed orange using FerroOrange dye; (j, k) Representative FITC fluorescence histogram plots and quantitative results of mean fluorescence intensity of cytosolic ROS in cells pre-treated with SB203580 using DCFH-DA dye; (l) Levels of TNF-α in cells pre-treated with SB203580. Data are showed as mean+SD of three independent experiments. (* P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001).

TFRC activated by nuclear translocated YAP1 mediated ferrous iron overload in A-GQDs-treated BV2 cells. (a, b, c) Gene and protein expressions of TFRC in cells transfected with LV-YAP1-siRNA-GFP and quantitative results of ratio of gray value; (d, e, f) Gene and protein expressions of TFRC in cells transfected with LV-YAP1-cDNA-GFP and quantitative results of ratio of gray value; (g, h) Representative fluorescent images of intracellular ferrous iron levels in cells transfected with LV-TFRC-siRNA-GFP (scale bar: 20 µm) and quantitative results of mean fluorescence intensity. The nucleus showed blue using DAPI, and ferrous iron accumulation showed orange using FerroOrange dye; (i, j) Representative PE fluorescence histogram plots and quantitative results of mean fluorescence intensity of cytosolic oxidative stress in cells transfected with LV-TFRC-siRNA-GFP using CellROX Orange dye; (k) The GSH/GSSG ratio in cells transfected with LV-TFRC-siRNA-GFP; (l) The cell viability of cells transfected with LV-TFRC-siRNA-GFP. Data are showed as mean+SD of three independent experiments. (* P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001).

ALOXE3 activated by nuclear translocated YAP1 regulated lipid peroxidation in A-GQDs-treated BV2 cells. (a, b, c) Gene and protein expressions of ALOXE3 in cells transfected with LV-YAP1-siRNA-GFP and quantitative results of ratio of gray value; (d, e, f) Gene and protein expressions of ALOXE3 in cells transfected with LV-YAP1-cDNA-GFP and quantitative results of ratio of gray value; (g) The cell viability of cells transfected with LV-ALOXE3-siRNA-GFP; (h, i) Representative PE fluorescence histogram plots and quantitative results of mean fluorescence intensity of cytosolic oxidative stress in cells transfected with LV-ALOXE3-siRNA-GFP using CellROX Orange dye; (j) The GSH/GSSG ratio; (k, l) Protein expression of ACSL4 and quantitative results of ratio of gray value; (m) The MDA content in cells transfected with LV-ALOXE3-siRNA-GFP. Data are showed as mean+SD of three independent experiments. (* P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001).

head.ce:title: YAP/TFRC/ALOXE3 signaling is involved in ferroptosis and neuroinflammation triggered by amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Toxicology & Key Laboratory of Environmental Toxicology of Anhui Higher Education Institutes, School of Public Health, Anhui Medical University, Hefei 230032, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been attracting extensive interests in a widespread application, but the little information on the risk assessments of GQDs could impose a restriction on their contributions in the neuroscience. In this study, the intranasal administration of amino group functionalized GQDs (A-GQDs) was observed to enhance the number of neuronal cell death, trigger ferroptosis activities, i.e. ferrous iron overload and lipid peroxidation as well as cause inflammatory reactions in hippocampus. Meanwhile, these adverse effects were found in BV2 microglial cells treated with A-GQDs, which were all attenuated by a ferroptosis-specific inhibitor ferrostain-1 (Fer-1). The inflammatory responses to A-GQDs in BV2 cells were evidenced as the increased secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokine TNF-α that was attributed to the high mobility group box 1 (HMGB1) released from ferroptosis-occurred cells. The nuclear transference of yes-associated protein (YAP), an effector in Hippo signaling pathway, regulated by the phosphorylation of p38 mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) was then found to play a critical role in A-GQDs triggering ferroptosis through transcriptionally mediating transferrin receptor protein (TFRC) and arachidonate lipoxygenase 3 (ALOXE3) to increase levels of ferrous iron and lipid peroxides, respectively. The findings not only highlight the importance of risk assessments on QDs containing low toxic component, but also provide toxic biomarkers for the interventions of GQDs in the brain.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a class of novel luminescent nanomaterials are planar nanocrystals of graphene fragments and display superior photoluminescence performance, which makes them promising in the applications of many fields, especially the biomedicine and neuroscience [1–3]. Moreover, once GQDs are functionalized at the surface or edge, the quantum yield of GQDs can be further enhanced to meet requirements of wilder usages. Recent studies have demonstrated that amino-functionalized GQDs (A-GQDs) are attracting a great deal of interest as excellent fluorescent probes in bioimaging, biosensing and drug delivery because the functionalized amino group makes GQDs get strong electron donation through primary amine molecules in order to exhibit intrinsic luminescence properties, especially in the near-infrared (NIR) region, and high photostability [4,5].

Along with the value of GQDs in the neuroscientific applications, their toxicity, especially in the central nervous system (CNS), is an area of growing interest regarding their safe use. Available studies suggested that inhaled carbon-based nanoparticles, including GQDs could translocate from nasal cavity to brain through the retrograde transport along the olfactory nerve [6–9], which could benefit to the nasal drug delivery system or neuroimaging technique, but be harmful to normal brains. Since the nanotoxicity of graphene have been extensively assessed during the past decades, GQDs with inherent graphene-like properties have been found to present toxicity in vitro and in vivo [10–12]. Recently, similar to most types of GQDs exhibiting toxicity in cultured cells and model animals [13–16], GQDs modified with amino group was found to cause some adverse effects in living bodies, particularly amount of reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation to damage nearby cells [4,14]. Our previous study also found the cytotoxicity of A-GQDs regarding oxidative stress damages which was slightly weaker than that of pristine GQDs with nitrogen doping [17]. Since brain in the CNS is susceptible to redox disequilibrium because of the possible lipid ROS productions originating from the rich in unsaturated fatty acids [18], the excessive ROS products caused by A-GQDs might result in severe brain injuries, but the available data on potential toxic effects of A-GQDs in the CNS is limited. Therefore, it is of importance to assess the neuro-biosafety of A-GQDs even though the amino group functionalization improved the biocompatibility of GQDs [4,14]. Otherwise, the lack information would undoubtedly restrict the applicability of A-GQDs in the field of neuroscience.

Recently, the induction effects produced by nanomaterials for ferroptosis were reported [19,20], and our previous study demonstrated the treatment of A-GQDs induced ferroptosis in cultured microglia that is a cell type plays an important role in the development and homeostatic of CNS [17]. Ferroptosis is a unique modality of cell death that is driven by iron-dependent phospholipid peroxidation and has been defined as a critical form of cell death in numerous organ injuries and degenerative pathologies, including neurodegenerative disorders [21,22]. Additionally, even though the links between ferroptosis with neuroinflammation remains unclear, ferroptosis have been reported to play physiological functions in triggering the innate immune system that can release pro-inflammatory cytokines and subsequently cause inflammation in the brain through damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), such as high mobility group box 1 (HMGB1) [23,24].

Recently, the intranasal administration of nanoparticles-loaded drug reaching the brain via olfactory bulb becomes a promising nasal nano-drug delivery for brain disorders [1,25]. In this study, experimental mice were intranasally instilled with A-GQDs to assess their potential adverse effects probably as a nasal delivery system in the diagnosis and treatment of brain disorders. The exposure dosages used here are based on concentrations of A-GQDs used for various biomedical applications in laboratory environments, such as drug delivery, photodynamic therapy and bioimages in consideration of A-GQDs not yet been licensed in the clinic [26–28]. Additionally, the data of different types of QDs in studies of ours and others all suggested that hippocampus was a main and critical target region of QDs entering into the brain, and exhibited severe injuries, mainly microglia-driven inflammatory reactions [29–33]. According to the microarray data, the role and potential mechanisms of the effector yes-associated protein (YAP) in Hippo signaling pathway in the mediation of A-GQDs causing ferroptosis and relevant inflammation in hippocampus of mice and cultured BV2 microglial cells were evaluated.

In nanotoxicological studies, it is important to consider the physicochemical properties of A-GQDs before conducting their bio-safety assessment due to the particularity of nanomaterials from common chemicals. The average particle size of A-GQDs analyzed by TEM images was uniformed and approximately 4 nm ( Figs. 1a and 1b), and the lattice spacing of crystal planes being 0.21 nm in HRTEM images (inserted photograph in Fig. 1a) matched the in-plan lattice spacing of graphite. The thickness of individual A-GQDs evaluated by AFM analysis was approximately 16 nm (Figs. 1c and 1d). The results of FI-IR spectrometry indicated that absorption peaks in the ranges of 3200–3500 cm-1 and 1650–1700 cm-1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of -OH and CO bounds for the aromatic domains, respectively, which showed the amino functional groups on the surface of A-GQDs (Fig. 1e).

Meantime, the hydrodynamic size and optical characteristics of A-GQDs were assessed in both deionized (DI) water and cell cultured medium in order to be consistent with the experimental environment when the polydispersity index (PDI) was 1.000 that indicated monodisperse distribution of GQDs in diluting solutions. The particle size distribution in Fig. 1b is based on the particle sizes measured by TEM where the nanoparticles are solid and uniformed. However, the hydrodynamic dimeter is based on nanoparticles dissolved in water and cell cultured medium where the particle sizes would become non-uniform due to influencing factors in water dispersion system, so the PDI is measured as 1.000.

The hydrodynamic dimeters of 100 µg/mL A-GQDs in DI water and culture medium were approximately 5.9 nm and 6.9 nm, respectively, which both were slightly larger than TEM size (Figs. 1f and 1g). The UV–vis absorption peaks of A-GQDs in two diluting solutions were in the spectral region of blue, but the emission slightly shifted to red from DI water to culture medium (Fig. 1h). A-GQDs presented obvious fluorescence spectra to show excellent photoluminescence (PL). The emission and excitation peaks of A-GQDs centered at the same wavelength in DI water and in culture medium, while the fluorescent intensity in water was slightly stronger than that in culture medium (Figs. 1i and 1j). The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of A-GQDs was approximately 20% in the quality report from the manufacture. More detailed physicochemical characteristics of A-GQDs showed in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A-GQDs triggered ferroptosis and caused neuroinflammation in the hippocampus of mice
ce:section.ce:para: The endotoxin in the 100 µg/mL A-GQDs was detected in order to remove possibly interfering factors of assessing the inflammatory responses to A-GQDs because the inflammation-inducing capability of nanomaterials could be attributed to the contamination by endotoxin [34]. The result of 0.0043 EU/mL was much lower than the detected limitation of 0.01 EU/mL, which was considered as endotoxin free (Fig. S1) [35]. The exposure administration was chosen as intranasal instillation because not only GQDs have been reported to enter into brain via the olfactory nerve starting from nasal cavity [6–9], but also the exposure route could mimic the probably GQDs-based nose-to-brain drug delivery to do the diagnosis and therapy of brain disorders [36,37]. Thus, the exposure concentrations were according to the neuroscientific applied dosages of A-GQDs in laboratory experiments that could extrapolate to human beings via using the safety factor when they have not yet been licensed in the clinic [4,38,39].

Firstly, the biodistribution and accumulation of intranasal exposed A-GQDs in the brain were assessed by the fluorescent intensity due to their bright fluorescence. The results suggested that the fluorescent intensity showed by radiant efficiency in the cortex and hippocampus of mice treated with A-GQDs were significantly higher than the control, which indicated that the cortex and hippocampus both were targets of A-GQDs in the brain (Fig. S2). However, since the volume of cortex is far larger than that of hippocampus but the radiant efficiency of cortex is not obviously higher than that of hippocampus, we decided that the hippocampus might be the primary brain region targeted by A-GQDs and should be investigated comprehensively.

No individual death, severe damages in the general health and the brain were observed in experimental animals of all groups (Fig. S3a and S3b), and the histomorphology of neurons and microglia in hippocampus did not remarkably change (Fig. S3c). These unchanged phenotypes not only suggested the low-toxicity of A-GQDs, but also guided us to save neurobehavioral experiments. However, the ultrastructure of neuronal cells and synapses altered at an adverse way, including dilated endoplasmic reticulum, broken mitochondrial ridge, shrinking mitochondria, and swollen postsynaptic membrane (Fig. S3d). Furthermore, the number of dead neuronal cells significantly increased in the hippocampus of mice treated with high-dosed A-GQDs ( Figs. 2a and 2b). Interestingly, damages in neuronal cells caused by A-GQDs could be efficiently reversed by a specific ferroptosis inhibitor Ferrostain-1 (Fer-1), which suggested that A-GQDs exposure might cause a novel cell death called ferroptosis in hippocampus.

When measuring typical characteristics of ferroptosis in the hippocampus of mice, results showed that the level of ferrous iron significantly increased after the high-dosed A-GQDs exposure when compared to the control (Fig. 2c and Fig. S3e). Meanwhile, the lipid peroxidation that was evidenced by the up-regulated expression of 4-HNE protein (Figs. 2d and 2e), increases in MDA content (Fig. S3f) and NADP+ /NADPH ratio (Fig. S3g) were all observed in the hippocampus of mice exposed to A-GQDs. Fer-1 was capable of alleviating both iron overload and lipid peroxidation induced by high-dosed A-GQDs exposure in vivo. As we known, sustained damages at the molecular level would certainly lead to phenotypical changes and the ferroptosis has been reported to play a critical role in hippocampal-related diseases [40]. Thus, the impairments of learning, memory and emotional responses that is closely related with hippocampal structure and function could occur after a chronic exposure of A-GQDs and would be assessed in the near further.

Except of A-GQDs triggering ferroptosis, the microglial activation evidenced by the increased expression of IBA1 was observed in the hippocampus of mice treated with A-GQDs (Fig. S4a∼S4d). In view of the importance of microglia in the neuroinflammation, we also found the inflammatory cell infiltration (Fig. 2 f and 2 g) and increased levels of inflammation-relevant cytokines of HMGB1 and TNF-α (Fig. 2h and 2i) occurred in the hippocampus of mice treated with the high-dosed A-GQDs, which might be related with enhancements in the number of inflammatory cells, such as white blood cell and neutrophil cell (Table S2) and the levels of HMGB1 and TNF-α (Fig. S4e and S4f) in the circular system. The treatment of Fer-1 was able to inhibit the microglial activation and neuroinflammation, which reminds us the close association between ferroptosis and neuroinflammation both caused by intranasal A-GQDs exposure in hippocampus. Since the neuroinflammation was mainly regulated by microglia and associated with/without ferroptosis has been confirmed to be a major player in the impairments of memory and cognitive function that are primary phenotypes of Alzheimer's disease [41,42], the long-term exposure of A-GQDs might affect the spatial memory of animals through the induction of ferroptosis and followed inflammation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A-GQDs triggered ferroptosis to increase inflammatory cytokines secretion in the cultured BV2 microglial cells
ce:section.ce:para: Microglia have been reported to participate in the neuroinflammatory responses to several types of carbon-based nanomaterials, and they have been found to be more sensitive to the toxicity of carbon-based nanomaterials than neurons [6,43]. Our pre-experiments also suggested that the cytotoxicity of A-GQDs regarding cell death, iron overload, excessive ROS generation and alterations in the expression of ferroptosis biomarkers was more obvious in microglial cells than hippocampus neuronal cells (Fig. S5), which emphasized the critical role of microglia in A-GQDs-caused damages. Thus, we speculated that the induction of ferroptosis and inflammation in microglia mainly contribute to the impairments in hippocampus of mice.

In this study, microglial BV2 cell line was used to investigate the molecular mechanisms of A-GQDs because it is the most commonly used microglial cell model to evaluate the neuro-inflammatory effects of nanomaterials due to having a similar phenotype with microglia [44], and the uptake of A-GQDs was observed in cultured BV2 microglial cells (Fig. S6a∼S6d). Some researchers have found that the GQDs exhibit toxicity in cultured cell lines at dosages above 60 µg/mL regardless of incubation time [45]. According to available studies, the delivery efficiency of nose-to-brain translocation is 0.1–2% [46], so it would be 0.01–0.2 µg/g in the brain when the intranasal administration dosage of A-GQDs is 1 mg/kg, which then multiplied accumulation coefficient (∼1000) to get internal exposure doses being 10–200 µg/g (approximately equal to 10–200 µg/mL). Furthermore, the treatment of A-GQDs at the dosages above 50 µg/mL for 24 h significantly decreased the cell viability compared to the control in BV2 cells when the cytotoxicity of A-GQDs was at a dose-dependent manner (Fig. S6e). Together with the study using A-GQDs to do bioimaging in living cells [47], the in vitro administration concentrations of A-GQDs were set as 25–100 µg/mL to assess the cytotoxicity regarding ferroptosis and inflammation when the dose-effect relationship of A-GQDs is not linear.

After the increased number of necrotic and late apoptotic cells were observed in BV2 cells treated with 25–100 µg/mL A-GQDs ( Figs. 3a and 3b), we also found the enhancements in the cytosolic ferrous iron levels (Figs. 3c and 3d) and cytosolic ROS generation (Figs. 3e and 3 f), as well as lipid peroxidation evidenced by increases in the cytosolic lipid ROS production (Fig. 3 g and 3 h), MDA contents (Fig. 3i) and GSH depletion (Fig. 3j) in BV2 cells treated with A-GQDs. Additionally, the expressions of two typical protein biomarkers of ferroptosis, i.e. GPX4 and SCL7A11, remarkably decreased in BV2 cells treated with A-GQDs (Figs. 3k and 3 l). Significantly, the disruption of iron metabolism, oxidative stress damage and lipid peroxidation became severe when the administration dosages increased.

Since the ferroptosis-relevant alternations induced by A-GQDs were remarkable when the exposure dose is 100 µg/mL, we evaluated the ultrastructure of BV2 cells exposed to 100 µg/mL A-GQDs and found some adversely changes, including ER dilation and mitochondria damages (Fig. 3 m). Similar to the protective role of Fer-1 in vivo, the pre-treatment of Fer-1 not only effectively alleviated the necrosis (Figs. 3a and 3b), but also significantly mitigated the iron overload, lipid peroxidation and alterations of biomarker protein expressions caused by A-GQDs in BV2 cells (Fig. 3c∼3o).

The microarray data in our previous study suggested that expressions of many genes associated with inflammation altered in BV2 cells treated with 100 µg/mL A-GQDs ( Fig. 4a). Several researchers have found that the exposure of carbon/graphene-based nanomaterials lead to inflammation through disturbing iron metabolism [48] or inducing M1 polarization of microphages [49]. Here, the levels of inflammatory mediator HMGB1 and pro-inflammatory cytokine TNF-α both increased in the culture medium of cells treated with A-GQDs, especially when the dosage was 100 µg/mL (Figs. 4b and 4c), which could be reversed by the pre-treatment of Fer-1 (Figs. 4d and 4e). The association between ferroptosis and neuroinflammation have been reported in several studies but the detailed mechanisms are not clear [24,50]. According to some studies showing that ferroptosis could induce DAMPs release and result in the recruitment of inflammatory cells and other inflammatory reactions [24,51], we assessed the causal relationship between the releases of a typical DAMP called HMGB1 and a pro-inflammatory cytokine TNF-α in BV2 cells treated with A-GQDs. The results suggested that the excessive secretions of TNF-α caused by A-GQDs were efficiently reversed when HMGB1 releases were inhibited by a HMGB1 inhibitor glycyrrhizin (Fig. S6f and Fig. 4f), which indicated that the inflammatory reaction to A-GQDs might be attributed to the HMGB1 released from ferroptotic BV2 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The YAP1 nuclear translocation mediated by p38 MAPK phosphorylation took part in the regulation of A-GQDs inducing ferroptosis and inflammation both in vitro and in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Our previous microarray data demonstrated that genes altered by A-GQDs were mostly enriched in the Hippo signaling pathway, which provided a hint that Hippo signaling pathway might be involved in mediating A-GQDs to trigger ferroptosis in BV2 cells ( Fig. 5a). Firstly, we evaluated the expressions of critical genes and proteins in the Hippo signaling pathway and found that YAP1 could be the toxic target of A-GQDs (Fig. 5b and Fig. S7a and S7b). Some researchers have reported that cells with a higher YAP expression are more sensitive to ferroptosis [52], and the activity of YAP is regulated by the state of phosphorylation and intracellular trans-localization [53]. In BV2 cells treated with A-GQDs, the WB results showed the expressions of p-YAP1 and YAP1 decreased in cytoplasm while the YAP1 expression increased in nucleus (Figs. 5c and 5d), and the IF images also showed increased YAP1 expression in nucleus (Figs. 5e and 5 f), which both indicated the nuclear translocation of YAP1. When BV2 cells were pre-treated with a YAP1-TEAD inhibitor peptide 17, A-GQDs-decreased cell viability were remarkably reversed (Fig. S7d).

We further used RNA interfering technique to knock down or over express the YAP1 expression to investigate the essential role of YAP1 in A-GQDs triggering ferroptosis. When the expression of YAP1 was inhibited in BV2 cells (Fig. S7e and S7f), the adverse effects associated with ferroptosis caused by 100 µg/mL A-GQDs, including decreased cell viability (Fig. S7g), increased necrosis (Fig. 5 g and 5 h), oxidative stress (Figs. 5i and 5j), ferrous iron overload (Figs. 5k and 5 l), GSH depletion (Fig. S7h) and increased MDA contents (Fig. S7i), as well as excessive pro-inflammatory cytokine TNF-α secretion (Fig. 5 m) were all significantly alleviated. On the contrary, when the YAP1 over expressed in BV2 cells (Fig. S7j and S7k), the cell death (Fig. S7l and Fig. 5 n and 5o), ferroptosis characteristics changes (Fig. 5p∼5 s and Fig. S7m and S7n) and inflammation (Fig. 5t) all aggravated after A-GQDs exposure, which could be efficiently reversed by Fer-1 (Fig. 5 n∼5 t and Fig. S7l∼S7n). Since some researchers found the combat of Hippo signaling pathway against inflammation, especially through its resistant effects in the inflammatory lesion [54], the nuclear transference of YAP1 induced by A-GQDs might contribute to the neuroinflammation independent on the ferroptosis pathway in BV2 cells.

Meanwhile, the nuclear translocation of YAP1 was also observed in the hippocampus of mice treated with A-GQDs (Fig. S8a and S8b). The microinjection of lentivirus was used to establish mice with YAP1 expression knock-down in the hippocampus (Fig. S8c∼S8f), and no death or severe brain damages were observed in these mice during the whole experiment (Fig. S8g∼S8j). When the mice microinjected with LV-YAP1-siRNA-GFP were intranasal administrated with A-GQDs, the neuronal cell death ( Figs. 6a and 6b), ferroptosis-related changes (Fig. 6c∼6e and Fig. S8k∼S8l), and neuroinflammatory reactions (Fig. 6 f∼6i and Fig. S8m∼S8n) were all milder than the control. Actually, it is still debatable on the actual anti-tumor effect of YAP-promoting ferroptosis. Researchers prefer to agree that YAP is critical for regulating both the initiation and progression of ferroptosis in their entire process where YAP exerts a tumor-suppressive effect but later an oncogenic effect [55]. However, our findings are consistent with the role of many transcriptional factors including YAP in regulating ferroptosis by affecting the expression of ferroptosis regulators at the early stage [56]. Further studies would focus on the effects of A-GQDs regulated by YAP in the progression of ferroptosis after a long-term exposure time.

When several studies have reported phosphorylated p38 (p-p38) MAPK could result in YAP up-regulation [57–59], we found the MAPK signaling were activated and p38 MAPK genes instead of ERK and JNK genes up-regulated in BV2 cells treated with A-GQDs (Fig. 5a, Fig. 7a). Meanwhile, the expression of p-p38 MAPK protein also increased while the protein expressions of p-ERK and p-JNK remained unchanged in cells treated with A-GQDs (Fig. 7b, Fig. S9a∼S9d). After the protein expressions of p-p38 and p38 were inhibited by a p38 MAPK inhibitor SB203580 (Fig. S9e and S9f), the nuclear translocation of YAP1 caused by A-GQDs were restrained as well (Figs. 7d and 7e). The increases in the necrosis cells (Fig. 7 f and 7 g), ferrous iron level (Fig. 7 h and 7i), cytosolic ROS generation (Figs. 7j and 7k) and TNF-α secretion (Fig. 7 l) were all reversed by the pre-treatment of SB203580 in BV2 cells exposed to A-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The nuclear transference of YAP1 caused by A-GQDs activated transferrin receptor protein (TFRC) to increase ferrous iron level in BV2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: Dephosphorylated YAP would localize into the nucleus to associate with transcriptional factors, like transcriptional-enhanced associated domain (TEAD) family proteins to drive expression of target genes encoding proteins that involved in the signaling to affect metabolisms of iron and lipid [52,60]. The microarray data suggested altered expressions of ferroptosis-related genes (Fig. S10a) and their relationship with YAP1 (Fig. S10b) in BV2 cells treated with 100 µg/mL A-GQDs, which indicated two proteins TFRC and arachidonate lipoxygenase 3 (ALOXE3) that both up-regulated in A-GQDs-exposed BV2 cells and hippocampus (Fig. S10c∼S10f) might be transcriptionally regulated by nuclear translocated YAP1. The expression of TFRC down-regulated in BV2 cells and hippocampus with the lentivirus transfection to knock down YAP1 and cells pretreated with p38 inhibitor ( Fig. 8a∼8c, Fig. S10g∼S10j, Fig. S9g and S9h), while the TFRC expression up-regulated in BV2 cells with overexpressed YAP1 (Fig. 8d∼8 f), which indicated that nuclear translocated YAP1 mediated by p38 MAPK is the key regulator of TFRC expression.

As we know, TFRC plays an important role in the iron absorption, so the activation of TFRC might result in iron overload following with oxidative stress caused by Fenton reaction [61–63]. In BV2 cells with the TFRC low-expression by transfecting with LV-TFRC-siRNA-GFP (Fig. S11a and S11b), the ferrous iron overload (Fig. 8 g and 8 h), oxidative stress (Figs. 8i and 8j) and GSH depletion (Fig. 8k) were all slighter than the control after A-GQDs exposure along with the decreased cell viability (Fig. 8 l). Similarly, the ferroptosis effects elicited by A-GQDs in BV2 cells with YAP1 over-expressed, including decreased cell viability (Fig. S12a), intracellular ferrous iron overload (Fig. S12b and S12c), oxidative stress (Fig. S12d and S12e) and GSH depletion (Fig. S12f) were all reversed by the pre-treatment of an iron chelator deferoxamine mesylate (DFOM). Apart from the reported direct impact of carbon-based nanomaterials on iron homeostasis, such as iron absorption, storage, and utilization [48], this study offered a biomarker regulated by a target (YAP1) of A-GQDs to disorder iron absorption and resulted in disturbance of iron distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The nuclear transference of YAP1 caused by A-GQDs activated ALOXE3 to elicit lipid peroxidation in BV2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: With the assistance of lentivirus transfection, ALOXE3 was assured to be a target biomolecule of YAP1 activated by p38 MAPK in cells and hippocampus treated with A-GQDs ( Fig. 9a∼9 f, Fig. S9k∼S9n, Fig. S9i and S9j). ALOXE3 have been found to mediate the lipid metabolism in most cells [64,65]. When the expression of ALOXE3 down-regulated in BV2 cells transfected with LV-ALOXE3-siRNA-GFP (Fig. S11c and S11d), the decreased cell viability (Fig. 9 g) and lipid peroxidation evidenced by oxidative stress (Fig. 9 h and 9i), GSH depletion (Fig. 9j), up-regulated ACSL4 expression (Figs. 9k and 9 l) and increased MDA content (Fig. 9 m) were all relieved when compared to the control under the A-GQDs exposure. Meanwhile, these adverse effects caused by A-GQDs were all alleviated by the pre-treatment of a total antioxidant Trolox in BV2 cells with YAP1 over-expression (Fig. S13). Therefore, the activation of ALOXE3 was another biomarker mediated by YAP1 to disturb lipid metabolism that contributed to ferroptosis triggered by A-GQDs in BV2 cells.

In this study, ferroptosis activities and inflammatory responses were observed both in the hippocampus of mice and cultured microglia exposed to A-GQDs, in which the Hippo/YAP signaling was involved the regulation of A-GQDs triggering ferroptosis. A-GQDs was found to promote the nuclear translocation of YAP1 by activating p38 MAPK that mediates the transcriptional outputs of two target genes tfrc encoding TFRC and aloxe3 encoding ALOXE3. The former one was activated to enhance the content of intracellular ferrous iron, while the later one up-regulated to produce excessive oxylipin, which became a combination of resulting in ferroptosis and relevant inflammation (Fig. S14). This study not only indicated the molecular mechanisms of YAP/TFRC/ALOXE3 pathway in GQDs inducing ferroptosis, but also reminded researchers to pay more attention on the mechanism-based risk assessments of low-toxic QDs.

Tianshu WU: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xinyu WANG: Formal analysis, Data curation. Min CHEN: Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiaomeng ZHANG: Formal analysis, Data curation. Jixiang ZHANG: Formal analysis, Data curation. Xuqing FAN: Validation. Yunbo YU: Validation. Wenxin ZHENG: Validation. Supu MAITIYV: Validation. Yán WĀNG: Writing – review & editing, Recourses. Lu KONG: Supervision. Meng TANG: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 82103884, 82103883) and Supporting Program of Southeast University Zhishan Young Scholar (grant number 2242021R41139).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nantod.2022.101703.
